--- title: "Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction" subtitle: "CPLN 5080 - Spring 2026" author: "Your Name" date: today format: html: code-fold: show code-tools: true toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-location: left theme: cosmo embed-resources: true editor: visual execute: warning: false message: false --- # INTRODUCTION ## The Rebalancing Challenge in Philadelphia Philadelphia's Indego bike share system faces the same operational challenge as every bike share system: **rebalancing bikes to meet anticipated demand**. Imagine you're an Indego operations manager at 6:00 AM on a Monday morning. You have: - 200 stations across Philadelphia - Limited trucks and staff for moving bikes - 2-3 hours before morning rush hour demand peaks - **The question:** Which stations will run out of bikes by 8:30 AM? This lab will teach you to build predictive models that forecast bike share demand across **space** (different stations) and **time** (different hours) to help solve this operational problem. ## Learning Objectives By the end of this assignment, you will be able to: 1. **Understand panel data structure** for space-time analysis 2. **Create temporal lag variables** to capture demand persistence 3. **Build multiple predictive models** with increasing complexity 4. **Validate models temporally** (train on past, test on future) 5. **Analyze prediction errors** in both space and time 6. **Engineer new features** based on error patterns 7. **Critically evaluate** when prediction errors matter most ## Assignment Structure **Part 2 (HW5 - Teams of 2):** - Work through Q3 2025 Indigo Bike-share data #SET UP ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidyverse) library(lubridate) library(sf) library(tigris) library(tidycensus) library(riem) # For Philadelphia weather from ASOS stations library(viridis) # color scales for visualization library(knitr) library(kableExtra) library(patchwork) library(here) options(scipen = 999) # Get rid of scientific notation to make it look tidier ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plotTheme <- theme_minimal() + theme( plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"), plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10), plot.caption = element_text(size = 8), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10, angle = 45, hjust = 1), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10), axis.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"), panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "#D0D0D0", linewidth = 0.2), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(), legend.position = "right" ) mapTheme <- theme_void() + theme( plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"), plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10), plot.caption = element_text(size = 8), legend.position = "right", plot.margin = margin(1, 1, 1, 1, "cm"), legend.key.height = unit(1, "cm"), legend.key.width = unit(0.2, "cm") ) palette5 <- c("#eff3ff", "#bdd7e7", "#6baed6", "#3182bd", "#08519c") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} census_api_key(Sys.getenv("CENSUS_API_KEY")) #Use the following code if you're not able to access the key in your environment #census_api_key("yourkeyhere", install=TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) ``` ::: # IMPORT AND PREP DATA ## Load Indego Trip Data (Q3 2025) **Note for HW5:** You'll download a different quarter from: https://www.rideindego.com/about/data/ ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Read Q3 2025 data indego <- read_csv(here("data/raw/indego-trips-2025-q3.csv")) bike_data<- st_read("C:/Users/dalan/Desktop/PPA_CPLN5920/GitHub/CPLN5920_Jalloh_Portfolio_2/labs/lab_5/data/raw/Bike_Network.shp") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Reading layer `Bike_Network' from data source `C:\Users\dalan\Desktop\PPA_CPLN5920\GitHub\CPLN5920_Jalloh_Portfolio_2\labs\lab_5\data\raw\Bike_Network.shp' using driver `ESRI Shapefile' Simple feature collection with 5225 features and 8 fields Geometry type: LINESTRING Dimension: XY Bounding box: xmin: -8378937 ymin: 4847835 xmax: -8345146 ymax: 4883978 Projected CRS: WGS 84 / Pseudo-Mercator ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Quick look at the data glimpse(indego) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 465,464 Columns: 15 $ trip_id 1203073573, 1203073759, 1203073753, 1203073877, 12… $ duration 10, 19, 16, 19, 2, 6, 20, 4, 52, 3, 31, 9, 10, 9, … $ start_time "7/1/2025 0:06", "7/1/2025 0:11", "7/1/2025 0:13",… $ end_time "7/1/2025 0:16", "7/1/2025 0:30", "7/1/2025 0:29",… $ start_station 3163, 3304, 3394, 3207, 3061, 3320, 3201, 3078, 32… $ start_lat 39.94974, 39.94234, 39.92400, 39.95441, 39.95425, … $ start_lon -75.18097, -75.15399, -75.16959, -75.19200, -75.17… $ end_station 3374, 3315, 3394, 3368, 3156, 3320, 3196, 3235, 32… $ end_lat 39.97280, 39.94235, 39.92400, 39.97647, 39.95381, … $ end_lon -75.17971, -75.21138, -75.16959, -75.17362, -75.17… $ bike_id "31674", "29473", "27889", "31825", "25400", "3172… $ plan_duration 30, 1, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30, 30,… $ trip_route_category "One Way", "One Way", "Round Trip", "One Way", "On… $ passholder_type "Indego30", "Walk-up", "Indego30", "Indego30", "In… $ bike_type "electric", "electric", "electric", "electric", "e… ``` ::: ::: ## Create Time Bins We need to aggregate trips into hourly intervals for our panel data structure. Read up on the lubridate library for more information on the date/time data extraction. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} indego <- indego %>% mutate( # Parse datetime start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time), end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time), # Create hourly bins interval60 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"), # Extract time features week = week(interval60), month = month(interval60, label = TRUE), dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE), hour = hour(interval60), date = as.Date(interval60), # Create useful indicators weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0), rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0) ) # Look at temporal features head(indego %>% select(start_datetime, interval60, week, dotw, hour, weekend)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 6 start_datetime interval60 week dotw hour weekend 1 2025-07-01 00:06:00 2025-07-01 00:00:00 26 Tue 0 0 2 2025-07-01 00:11:00 2025-07-01 00:00:00 26 Tue 0 0 3 2025-07-01 00:13:00 2025-07-01 00:00:00 26 Tue 0 0 4 2025-07-01 00:16:00 2025-07-01 00:00:00 26 Tue 0 0 5 2025-07-01 00:16:00 2025-07-01 00:00:00 26 Tue 0 0 6 2025-07-01 00:18:00 2025-07-01 00:00:00 26 Tue 0 0 ``` ::: ::: ![](images/clipboard-9133593.png) #EDA OF INDEGO DATA ## Trips Over Time ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Daily trip counts daily_trips <- indego %>% group_by(date) %>% summarize(trips = n()) ggplot(daily_trips, aes(x = date, y = trips)) + geom_line(color = "#3182bd", linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q3 2025", subtitle = "Summer-Fall demand patterns in Philadelphia", x = "Date", y = "Daily Trips", caption = "Source: Indego bike share" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Part_3_files/figure-html/trips_over_time-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ``` ``` ## Hourly Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Average trips by hour and day type hourly_patterns <- indego %>% group_by(hour, weekend) %>% summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>% mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday")) ggplot(hourly_patterns, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) + labs( title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns", subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays", x = "Hour of Day", y = "Average Trips per Hour", color = "Day Type" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Part_3_files/figure-html/hourly_patterns-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Question:** When are the peak hours? ![](Part_3_files/figure-html/map_philly-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Question** These data are in WGS84. If you wanted to calculate a spatial distance-based variable, like a buffer, should you use the dataset as it? ## Join Census Data to Stations We'll spatially join census characteristics to each bike station. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create sf object for stations stations_sf <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326) #Bike Data stations_sf <- st_transform(stations_sf, st_crs(bike_data)) d<- st_distance(stations_sf, bike_data) avg_3nn <- apply(d, 1, function(x) { mean(sort(as.numeric(x))[1:3]) }) stations_sf <- stations_sf %>% mutate(avg_3nn_bikelane_dist = avg_3nn) %>% arrange(desc(avg_3nn_bikelane_dist)) stations_sf <- st_transform(stations_sf, st_crs(bike_data)) philly_census<- st_transform(philly_census, st_crs(stations_sf)) # Spatial join to get census tract for each station stations_census <- st_join(stations_sf, philly_census, left = TRUE) %>% st_drop_geometry() # Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts. stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Add back to trip data indego_census <- indego %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop, Median_age, avg_3nn_bikelane_dist), by = "start_station" ) # Prepare data for visualization stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Create the map showing problem stations ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = "white", size = 0.1) + scale_fill_viridis( option = "viridis", name = "Median\nIncome", labels = scales::dollar, na.value = "grey90" ) + # Stations with census data (small grey dots) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6 ) + # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(!has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5 ) + labs( title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract", subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)", caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts" ) + mapTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Part_3_files/figure-html/join_census_to_stations-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Dealing with missing data We need to decide what to do with the non-residential bike share stations. For this example, we are going to remove them -- this is not necessarily the right way to do things always, but for the sake of simplicity, we are narrowing our scope to only stations in residential neighborhoods. We might opt to create a separate model for non-residential stations.. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Identify which stations to keep valid_stations <- stations_census %>% filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) %>% pull(start_station) # Filter trip data to valid stations only indego_census <- indego %>% filter(start_station %in% valid_stations) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop, Median_age, avg_3nn_bikelane_dist), by = "start_station" ) ``` ::: ## Get Weather Data Weather significantly affects bike share demand! Let's get hourly weather for Philadelphia. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Get weather from Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL) # This covers Q3 2025: July 1 - September 30 weather_data <- riem_measures( station = "PHL", # Philadelphia International Airport date_start = "2025-07-01", date_end = "2025-09-30" ) # Process weather data weather_processed <- weather_data %>% mutate( interval60 = floor_date(valid, unit = "hour"), Temperature = tmpf, # Temperature in Fahrenheit Precipitation = ifelse(is.na(p01i), 0, p01i), # Hourly precip in inches Wind_Speed = sknt # Wind speed in knots ) %>% select(interval60, Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed) %>% distinct(interval60, .keep_all = TRUE) # Check for missing hours and interpolate if needed weather_complete <- weather_processed %>% complete(interval60 = seq(min(interval60), max(interval60), by = "hour")) %>% fill(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed, .direction = "down") # Look at the weather summary(weather_complete %>% select(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Temperature Precipitation Wind_Speed Min. :57.00 Min. :0.0000000 Min. : 0.000 1st Qu.:70.00 1st Qu.:0.0000000 1st Qu.: 4.000 Median :76.00 Median :0.0000000 Median : 6.000 Mean :75.94 Mean :0.0008342 Mean : 6.218 3rd Qu.:81.00 3rd Qu.:0.0000000 3rd Qu.: 8.000 Max. :98.00 Max. :0.1000000 Max. :25.000 ``` ::: ::: ## Visualize Weather Patterns Who is glad it's Spring!? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(weather_complete, aes(x = interval60, y = Temperature)) + geom_line(color = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.7) + geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red") + labs( title = "Philadelphia Temperature - Q3 2025", subtitle = "Summer to early fall transition", x = "Date", y = "Temperature (°F)" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Part_3_files/figure-html/visualize-weather-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # CREATE SPACE-TIME PANEL ## Aggregate Trips to Station-Hour Level ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Count trips by station-hour trips_panel <- indego_census %>% group_by(interval60, start_station, start_lat, start_lon, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop, Median_age, avg_3nn_bikelane_dist) %>% summarize(Trip_Count = n()) %>% ungroup() # How many station-hour observations? nrow(trips_panel) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 214178 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # How many unique stations? length(unique(trips_panel$start_station)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 263 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # How many unique hours? length(unique(trips_panel$interval60)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2208 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} hist(trips_panel$Trip_Count) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Part_3_files/figure-html/aggregate-trips-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Create Complete Panel Structure Not every station has trips every hour. We need a **complete panel** where every station-hour combination exists (even if Trip_Count = 0). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Calculate expected panel size n_stations <- length(unique(trips_panel$start_station)) n_hours <- length(unique(trips_panel$interval60)) expected_rows <- n_stations * n_hours cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows, big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Expected panel rows: 580,704 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Current rows: 214,178 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Missing rows:", format(expected_rows - nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output :6312 NA's :6312 ``` ::: ::: # CREATE TEMPORAL LAG VARIABLES The key innovation for space-time prediction: **past demand predicts future demand**. ## Why Temporal Lags? If there were 15 bike trips from Station A at 8:00 AM, there will probably be \~15 trips at 9:00 AM. We can use this temporal persistence to improve predictions. **Note:** We still can't use spatial lags in prediction. Temporal lags are different because time moves in one direction - past predicts the future, but the future does not predict the past. Spatial lags are non-directional and create circular prediction logic. Temporal lags: good Spatial lags: bad! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sort by station and time study_panel <- study_panel %>% arrange(start_station, interval60) # Create lag variables WITHIN each station study_panel <- study_panel %>% group_by(start_station) %>% mutate( lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count, 1), lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 2), lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 3), lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 12), lag1day = lag(Trip_Count, 24) ) %>% ungroup() # Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station) study_panel_complete <- study_panel %>% filter(!is.na(lag1day)) cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows after removing NA lags: 574,392 ``` ::: ::: ## Visualize Lag Correlations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sample one station to visualize example_station <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>% head(168) # One week # Plot actual vs lagged demand ggplot(example_station, aes(x = interval60)) + geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + scale_color_manual(values = c( "Current" = "#08519c", "1 Hour Ago" = "#3182bd", "24 Hours Ago" = "#6baed6" )) + labs( title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station", subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand", x = "Date-Time", y = "Trip Count", color = "Time Period" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Part_3_files/figure-html/lag_correlations-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # TEMPORAL TRAIN/TEST SPLIT **CRITICAL:** We must train on PAST data and test on FUTURE data! ## Why Temporal Validation Matters In real operations, at 6:00 AM on March 15, we need to predict demand for March 15-31. We have data from Jan 1 - March 14, but NOT from March 15-31 (it hasn't happened yet!). **Wrong approach:** Train on weeks 10-13, test on weeks 1-9 (predicting past from future!) **Correct approach:** Train on weeks 1-9, test on weeks 10-13 (predicting future from past) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Split by week # Q1 has weeks 27-39 (Jul-Oct) # Train on weeks 27-35 # Test on weeks 36-39 # Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods? early_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 36) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) late_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 36) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) # Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods common_stations <- intersect(early_stations, late_stations) # Filter panel to only common stations study_panel_complete <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(start_station %in% common_stations) # NOW create train/test split train <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 36) test <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 36) cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training observations: 397,656 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(test), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing observations: 176,736 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Training date range:", min(train$date), "to", max(train$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training date range: 20271 to 20333 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing date range:", min(test$date), "to", max(test$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing date range: 20334 to 20361 ``` ::: ::: #BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS We'll build 5 models with increasing complexity to see what improves predictions. ## Model 1: Baseline (Time + Weather) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding train <- train %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"))) # Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables) contrasts(train$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7) # Now run the model model1 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation, data = train ) summary(model1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation, data = train) Residuals: Min 1Q Median 3Q Max -1.7607 -0.7740 -0.2103 0.1990 21.0953 Coefficients: Estimate Std. The neighborhood with the highest errors is Errors seem to be clustered in the center-city/ and west Philadelphia areas of the city. These locations are also where demand is most concentrated. This indicates that our model has a harder time predicting ridership in higher demand areas. This is likely because bike ridership in center-city can be sporadic with infrequent patterns, making it harder to predict. The error can also be attributed to a spatial relationship that is not taken into account in a regular OLS regression. One potential feature that could be missing is average distance per trip. I hypothesize that bike trips that begin in center city are shorter than those that originate in other areas of the city, and could be added to the model as a predictor. 2. **Temporal patterns:** - When are errors highest? - Do certain hours/days have systematic under/over-prediction? - Are there seasonal patterns? It appears that errors are highest when predicting overnights, evenings and mid-day. The model does a better job at predicting AM rush which is indicated by the closer clustering of individual residual points and a slope that is closer in direction and strength as the red line. In general, the model has a harder time predicting a higher number of ridership regardless of the time of day. 3. **Demographic patterns:** - Relate errors to census characteristics - Are certain communities systematically harder to predict? - What are the equity implications? The model predicts better in area with more transit usage. This is indicated by the closer clustering of points along the red line and the error points that start to dissipate as train ridership increases. This is likely because travel patterns are more consistent among those who take transit especially if they rent the indego bikes to solve their last-mile problem. The model predicts worse in census tract with a higher percentage of white residents. Census tracts that have more white residents tend to be further Northeast or Northwest in Philadelphia where indego bikes are not serviced. The model also had a hard time predicting bike ridership with the median income of the census tract it was located in. This is likely because bike ridership is not associated with a certain income. Anyone of any income can use the indego bikes, albeit for different distances and different purposes.
